reasoning:
The baseline probability of an early PM ousting even amidst unpopularity is usually low due to internal resistance, but the situation for Keir Starmer in April 2026 is uniquely severe. Polling has collapsed to 20%, approval is firmly around -50 to -60, and Labour recently fell to third place in a historic by-election loss in Gorton & Denton (won by the Greens). A major scandal involving Peter Mandelson and Jeffrey Epstein ties has deeply fractured the party, with senior figures like Scottish Labour leader Anas Sarwar publicly demanding Starmer's resignation. 

Crucially, the May 7, 2026 local elections are universally cited as the tipping point. Projections suggest catastrophic losses (e.g., losing over 600 seats in London alone). Although Labour rules require 81 MPs to officially trigger a leadership contest, the unprecedented panic over dropping to 20% in the polls less than two years after a landslide will likely easily clear this threshold or trigger mass cabinet resignations (the "Cabinet Route" used to oust Johnson). Potential successors like Wes Streeting and Andy Burnham are already actively positioning. While Starmer has shown extreme tenacity (barring Burnham from standing, suspending critics), the structural pressure post-May makes it highly probable (around 72%) that he will be forced out or resign before the end of 2026. The probabilities trend toward 0.5 per the prompt's instructions for unspecified future resolution dates.
